original_abstract: This article explores the missional role of the Holy Spirit through the lens of Ghanaian Pentecostal theology and practice. Utilizing the concept of Missio Dei—specifically a trinitarian approach to mission—as a point of departure, the study focuses on the indispensable work of the Holy Spirit in the expansion of the church. Central to this discussion are Ghanaian Pentecostal perspectives on the baptism and infilling of the Spirit, alongside the lived ecclesial practices that stem from these beliefs. The article concludes that the church’s vocational success is entirely dependent upon the Holy Spirit, who both convicts the world of sin and empowers believers with the spiritual gifts necessary to boldly proclaim the gospel.
humanized_abstract: Article PDF Abstract This article delves into the missional role of the Holy Spirit within the context of Ghanaian Pentecostal understanding and practice. Departing from the theory of Missio Dei with its trinitarian nuances towards understanding mission, it scrutinizes the work of the Holy Spirit toward the global proliferation of the Christian church. Using Ghanaian Pentecostal perceptions of the baptism in the Holy Spirit and being filled with the Holy Spirit along with resultant ecclesial practices as a hermeneutic, this article concludes that the churches' vocational success hinges entirely on the Holy Spirit as it serves to convict humanity of its sinful state, and enable believers with suitable spiritual gifts so they can confront and preach the Gospel fearlessly.

document.input: This article explores the missional role of the Holy Spirit through the lens of Ghanaian Pentecostal theology and practice. Utilizing the concept of Missio Dei—specifically a trinitarian approach to mission—as a point of departure, the study focuses on the indispensable work of the Holy Spirit in the expansion of the church. Central to this discussion are Ghanaian Pentecostal perspectives on the baptism and infilling of the Spirit, alongside the lived ecclesial practices that stem from these beliefs. The article concludes that the church’s vocational success is entirely dependent upon the Holy Spirit, who both convicts the world of sin and empowers believers with the spiritual gifts necessary to boldly proclaim the gospel.
